questions.scenario: The checkout service generates customer receipt PDFs after payment. Support needs to download a receipt by order ID, the app stores metadata in SQL, and the PDF itself is never queried with SQL joins. Which Azure storage choice fits the file best?

options.text: Blob Storage, because the PDF behaves like a durable object that can be downloaded later.
Azure SQL Database, because every checkout-related value should live in the order database.
Cosmos DB, because a receipt can be represented as a JSON document.
A runtime-local folder, because the app can recreate the PDF if the container restarts.

questions.explanation: A receipt PDF is file-like data. Blob Storage is built for durable objects, while SQL can keep metadata such as order ID, blob name, and status.

questions.scenario: The storage account for receipts is `stdevpolarisordersprod`. The team is preparing a production review before exposing download links to customers. Which settings matter for the first safety check?

options.text: Public blob access is disabled unless the team has a deliberate public download design.
HTTPS-only access and a modern minimum TLS version are enabled.
The storage account has a lifecycle policy that matches the retention expectation for exports or temporary files.
The account name contains the exact word receipt so every engineer can guess its purpose.

questions.explanation: The safety check is about access, transport, and lifecycle. Naming helps humans, but it does not replace the security and retention settings.
